---
title: "Package_Details"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Package_Details}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  collapse = TRUE,
  comment = "#>"
)
```

```{r setup}
library(fatherStay)
```

## Objective

Our data are expected to look something like the following:

| date | y   | x1  | z1  | z2  |
|------|-----|-----|-----|-----|
| 1    | 1.1 | 0.5 | 2.3 | 1.2 |
| 2    | 1.5 | 0.2 | 2.7 | 1.2 |
| 3    | NA  | 0.1 | 2.8 | 1.5 |
| 4    | 1.5 | NA  | 2.7 | 1.3 |
| 5    | 1.8 | 0.2 | 2.9 | 1.2 |
| ...  | ... | ... | ... | ... |
| 204  | 2.5 | 1.2 | 1.7 | 2.2 |
| 205  | 2.9 | 0.8 | 0.7 | 2.5 |
| 206  | NA  | 0.2 | 2.7 | 2.9 |
| 207  | NA  | 0.6 | 2.2 | 1.9 |
| 209  | NA  | 0.4 | 2.0 | 3.9 |
| 210  | NA  | 0.2 | 1.0 | 3.8 |

Note the string of NA values in y in the last days of the study, which are the values to be nowcasted. Note also the presence of NA values in the response and the predictors.

## Imputation

We expect there to be some missing data in the predictor variables. Imputation is the best way to deal with them. The goal is that we will have relatively simple methods to impute the values of the explanatory variables $x_1$, $z_1$, and $z_2$, and the package will allow the user to specify the type of imputation to be used. Additionally our data has the advantage of having multiple potentially correlated variables. We would ideally like to use this fact to our advantage when imputing the data. Imputation is not the main focus of this project and so we will be keeping the imputation methods used relatively simple. 

There are several existing R packages that allow for imputation and work in a time series context, and while we do not want the program we are developing to be dependent on many packages it makes sense to use an existing package for the imputation since it is not a focus of the project. 

The first is imputeTS, a package in R for imputing univariate time series. It has the advantage of being specifically for time series data. It features a variety of complex methods for imputation such as; Kalman smoothing, and interpolation, as well as simple methods like using the last observation or the mean. There is really only one main disadvantage of this package, it does not take advantage of the multivariate nature of our data, and as a result other packages and imputation methods may do a better job than imputeTS.

The package that we will be using is MICE (multivariate imputation using chained equations), this is a widely used package (http://arxiv.org/abs/2402.04059). It uses chained equations to impute the missing values, this allows for imputation to take advantage of the natural structure that comes from time series data. The MICE package has several nice features that we will be taking advantage of in this project, and it integrates well with the types of functions we plan on creating and using in this project. The core idea behind the chained equations approach is that you find iterative conditional probabilities. That is we predict an imputed value and then use that prediction to calculate future predictions. We then iterate on this process to improve on these estimates.

## Nowcasting

Starting with defining the specific problem that we are wanting to solve. When referring to nowcasting we denote the measurement of DAD at time $t$ by the value $Y_t$ and the measurements of the explanatory variables at time $t$ to be a vector of values $X_{j,t}$ where $j \in \{1,2,3\}$ represents CNISP, PTSOS, and RVDSS. Then the problem that we are working is how to predict the value of $Y_t$ given the values $X_{j,t}$ and $Y_{t-1}$.

We will be making several key assumptions in this project. The first is that the data for the explanatory variables can be at least somewhat successfully imputed by reasonable estimates. We will also assume that we have access to all the updated values of the explanatory variables at the time $t$ when we are making a prediction for $Y_t$. Devan, is this a good/valid assumption to make?
